data.title: U.S. FDA Declares Shortage of Novo Nordisk's Wegovy, Ozempic Over — Update
data.description:
By Cristina Gallardo

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration declared that the shortage of Novo Nordisk's weight-loss medications Wegovy and Ozempic has been resolved, according to its website.

The FDA announcement confirms that the U.S. supply of both prescription-only drugs now meets or exceeds the current and projected demand in the country, Danish pharmaceutical company Novo Nordisk said Friday.

The popularity of Novo Nordisk's blockbuster GLP-1 drugs had forced the FDA to include Wegovy and Ozempic in its official shortage list, despite the company's efforts to ramp up production.

Ozempic, which treats diabetes, and Wegovy, sold for obesity, have spent over two years on the FDA's list. During that time, compounding pharmacies--those which produce custom medications--were legally entitled to produce copies of both semaglutide drugs, which were also cheaper than Novo Nordisk's versions.

The regulator's decision to take them off of the shortage list means that compounding pharmacies will need to stop making and selling knockoffs.

Novo Nordisk said that it continues to produce Wegovy and Ozempic 24 hours a day, seven days a week, in order to meet demand.

"No one should have to compromise their health due to misinformation and reach for fake or illegitimate knock-off drugs that pose significant safety risks to patients," said Dave Moore, executive vice president for U.S. Operations at Novo Nordisk.

Shares in Novo Nordisk are up 5.8% at 630 Danish kroner in late afternoon trade in Europe. The stock is up 0.9% year-to-date.

Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com

data.title: Canada Retail Sales Slide After Jumping 2.5% in December — Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Canadians look to have pared back spending at the start of the year, tightening their belts after flocking to retailers in December and driving up sales at the fastest rate in roughly two and half years.

Sales fell an estimated 0.4% in January, based on Statistics Canada's advance look at retail receipts.

That pullback, despite consumers still benefiting from a temporary federal tax break, may suggest shoppers took a breather and snapped a six-month run of rising sales. Or it could hint at some hesitancy given clouds over Canada's economy and threats of a trade dispute with the U.S.

Statistics Canada offered no details with the estimate for January, which was based on the responses of roughly 58% of retailers surveyed and will be revised.

The retreat in January only partly retraced a stronger-than-expected rise in sales the month before, and a lull was expected by economists after the typically busy Christmas period.

"We'll need to wait to see if the year-end optimism truly faded to start 2025," Bank of Montreal senior economist Shelly Kaushik said.

Data shows goods prices ticked up in January, and Kaushik said retail-sales volumes could still come in roughly flat for the month.

Consumers may have been encouraged to continue spending in the early part of the year by the two-month suspension of the goods-and-services tax on items including children's clothing, toys and restaurant meals, which came to an end mid-February.

Royal Bank of Canada's analysis of anonymized card transactions by Canadian customers showed significantly less was spent in January on clothing and electronics even as grocery baskets got a little lighter. One bright spot in the lender's January data was home-related spending on furniture and household electronics, as well as on building materials.

Tony Stillo, director of Canadian economics at Oxford Economics, said the tax holiday along with recent rebates given out by Ontario's government to households in the country's most populous province are likely to support higher spending in the first quarter of the year, but there are question marks beyond that. "We'll have to wait and see the extent to which heightened uncertainty from Trump's threatened trade war with Canada causes households to curtail spending, especially for big-ticket items," he said.

Sales in December surged 2.5% from the month before to a seasonally adjusted 69.59 billion Canadian dollars, the equivalent of about US$49.09 billion. Compared with a year earlier, retailers saw a 3.9% jump in sales in the last month of 2024.

The performance was much stronger than the 1.6% advance economists expected and the data agency had predicted. There were gains in each of the retail segments tracked by Statistics Canada and in each province, and overall sales volumes also were up a solid 2.5% for the month.

After sales for November were revised higher, it left growth in retail trade in the final quarter of last year accelerating to 2.4% following the weakness in the first two quarters of 2024.

Motor-vehicle and parts dealers were a big driver for the month, with new-car sales up for a third consecutive month. Sales at gas stations and fuel vendors also showed a big increase thanks to higher prices.

Core sales, which strip out car and auto-parts dealers and gas stations, were up 2.5% after weakness in November.

Food-and-beverage retailers also helped drive sales, with strong spending at supermarkets and at beer, wine and liquor stores. Higher receipts were also recorded at general merchandise retailers and clothing, accessories and leather-goods retailers in December.

A string of six interest-rate cuts by the Bank of Canada since June has lowered borrowing costs and helped drive household spending, offsetting weakness in business investment. The federal tax holiday helped keep price pressures in check in January, with annual inflation staying below the central bank's 2% target despite picking up slightly thanks to rising energy costs.

Still, the outlook for Canada's economy has become more uncertain under the looming threat from the White House of hefty tariffs on Canadian exports and President Trump's repeated calls for the country to become the 51st U.S. state. Business surveys indicate companies are more hesitant, with some delaying investment decisions and looking at supply chains in case tariffs hit.

"Consumer spending was clearly improving during the second half of 2024, even before accounting for the temporary boost in December from the GST holiday. However, recent tariff uncertainty may have resulted in households tightening the purse strings again if there was concern regarding employment prospects," Andrew Grantham, senior economist at CIBC Capital Markets, said.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: 'Chokepoints' Review: The Dollar on Defense
data.description:
By Daniel Yergin

"You f — ing Americans. Who are you to tell us, the rest of the world, that we're not going to deal with Iranians?" So exploded a British banker when warned that the U.S. would be applying sanctions to international banks doing business with Iran. As it turned out, that's exactly what the Americans did — hitting the defiant banker's own institution, among others, with hundreds of millions of dollars in fines.

In "Chokepoints: American Power in the Age of Economic Warfare," Edward Fishman makes clear why this banker so badly underestimated America's financial power. Deftly written, "Chokepoints" is a compelling and dramatic narrative about the new shape of geopolitics — one in which the U.S. mobilizes its economic and financial pre-eminence for geopolitical objectives, especially in its clashes with China, Iran and Russia. It's the story of a world economy that has moved from confident globalization to increasing fragmentation and in which economic warfare has become "a baseline feature of our world."

Mr. Fishman, who has held positions in the State, Defense and Treasury departments and now teaches at Columbia University's School of International and Public Affairs, has written an engrossing account of contemporary history. He provides a framework for understanding the battles to come as well as the growing challenges and risks facing companies that operate in the global economy.

Of course, there's nothing new about economic warfare. Athens in the fifth century B.C. deployed its mighty navy to impose a trade embargo on the city-state of Megara. The blockade succeeded but also backfired, helping precipitate a war with Sparta that would shatter Athens's golden age.

With the establishment of the League of Nations after World War I, Woodrow Wilson prophesied that the organization's ability to impose economic sanctions on aggressors would be "something more tremendous than war." As it turned out, sanctions did not impede Japan's conquest of Manchuria in 1931, Mussolini's attack on Ethiopia in 1935 nor the advent of World War II.

Mr. Fishman argues that "what makes today's economic wars novel is the highly interdependent world economy." He writes: "Great powers once rose and survived by controlling geographic chokepoints like the Bosphorus. American power in the globalized economy relies on chokepoints of a different kind." Foremost among them is the primacy of the dollar — in global trade, investments and financial transactions — and the role in the global economy played by the major U.S. banks. So much of the world's cross-border commerce, whether initially priced in rupees, riyals or pesos, is settled in dollars passing primarily through the U.S. financial system. "The U.S. dollar is involved in nearly 90 percent of foreign exchange transactions," Mr. Fishman notes. He cites one other critical chokepoint: "The intellectual property and technical know-how that underpin a vast array of essential technologies, notably the advanced computer chips at the core of the digital economy." With its command over these essential chokepoints, the U.S. wields its powerful "arsenal of economic weapons" — sanctions, export controls and investment restrictions.

Mr. Fishman dates the origin of this new age to the aftermath of 9/11 and the George W. Bush administration's drive to throttle terrorist funding. But the real catalyst, the author argues, was Iran. To halt Tehran's march to a nuclear bomb, Congress passed a series of laws enabling the Treasury Department to apply sanctions: Any financial institution found to be doing business with Iran risked being cut off from accessing the U.S. dollar, prevented from participating in the U.S. financial system and subject to huge fines. In the battle against the mullahs, Treasury was thus transformed from its traditionally supporting role to a central one.

Later, under the Obama administration, the threat of sanctions and the specter of huge fines persuaded banks and companies around the world to sever or shrink their economic ties with Iran. Purchases of Iranian oil plummeted. Iran's economy cratered, finally forcing Tehran to the negotiating table. A nuclear deal would be reached in 2015, though the first Trump administration would withdraw the U.S. as a signatory in 2018.

Russia has proved a more formidable foe in economic warfare. After Vladimir Putin seized Crimea in 2014, the U.S., in concert with its allies, cut off Russian access to credit markets and oil and gas technology; they also imposed travel bans on and froze the assets of Russians close to Mr. Putin. The Russian economy was sent into a tailspin. Eight years later, as Russian troops massed on the border with Ukraine, the Biden administration warned of the "most severe sanctions that have ever been imposed." Yet Mr. Putin still invaded Ukraine. The economic consequences of U.S. sanctions were not enough to dissuade Mr. Putin.

As Mr. Fishman explains, three factors contributed to Russia's resilience. First, once Russia met resistance to its invasion of Ukraine, the Kremlin pivoted to a wartime economy that continued to generate economic growth, albeit of a distorted kind, prioritizing military production at the expense of the civilian sector. Rampant inflation forced Russia's central bank to set interest rates at more than 20%. Second, in the face of sanctions from the West, Russia turned to China to fill the breach. As Mr. Fishman writes, "Sino-Russian trade flourished," with China providing everything from technology and civilian goods to bullet-proof vests. Russia is now economically dependent on China.

The third factor was the nature of the U.S. sanctions. While they targeted both finance and technology, they did not disrupt Russia in its greatest vulnerability — oil exports. Oil and gas revenues make up as much as 40% of Russia's total budget, and a cutoff of oil exports would have strangled the Russian economy. But here was a dilemma: Russia was exporting five times as much oil as Iran to the world market. The Biden administration feared that an embargo would have sent oil prices soaring, along with the price of gasoline at the pump. That would have been bad for the global economy and bad for U.S. politics.

The solution was a price cap that sought to set a maximum price of $60 a barrel on Russian oil, enforced by international sanctions and the fact that the global oil trade is immersed in a dollar-based web of shipping, insurance and marine services. But the 10 months it took for the U.S. and its allies to work out the price cap allowed Moscow enough time to cobble together a ghost fleet of hundreds of secondhand tankers and a network of shadowy traders who operate outside the dollar-based system. Russia was thus able to develop a parallel trading system and sell oil above the $60 cap, most notably to China and India.

Even though the sanctions have not stopped the war in Ukraine, they have inflicted damage upon Russia, impeding its imperial ambitions by cutting off its economy from the Western world. And last month, only 10 days before leaving office, the Biden administration — no longer concerned about the political fallout from rising oil prices — finally put in place tougher sanctions that can partially constrain Russia's ability to produce oil.

Today the most consequential battle of economic warfare is the one unfolding between China and the U.S. On the American side it reflects, Mr. Fishman tells us, a decided shift in thinking. The U.S. no longer believes that expanding economic relations with China will create common interests and international stability. Instead, the dominant view in America now is that China has been "waging an economic assault against the United States for decades," stealing technology and intellectual property, launching cyberattacks, seeking "to seize the commanding heights of the digital economy" and rapidly building up a military to push the U.S. back across the Pacific.

And so when U.S. intelligence warned that the Chinese telecommunications company Huawei was rolling out advanced communications systems around the world with a hidden danger — a "backdoor" that could enable Beijing to download confidential data at will — the first Trump administration convinced companies in the U.S. and its ally countries to swear off Huawei. Sanctions followed, denying Huawei access to advanced American computer chips. Under the Biden administration, this approach evolved into a policy of denying such chips to all Chinese companies.

The Biden administration's stated objective was to ensure that the U.S. maintains "as large of a lead as possible" on critical technologies, restraining China's ability to harness artificial intelligence and thus hinder its rapid military buildup. The U.S. campaign is backed by a drive to shift supply chains and investment away from China. Despite its many other differences with the first Trump administration, the Biden administration not only maintained continuity with its predecessor in its stance on China but further tightened the chokepoints.

Beijing decries these efforts as a campaign to "contain China" and is pushing back. Some argue that the vitality of the U.S. tech sector will ensure that a five-year gap between U.S. and Chinese technologies can be maintained. Others argue that China is pouring huge resources into shortening the gap and building up its own capabilities. According to some estimates, China awards more than 10 times as many engineering degrees a year as the U.S. Confidence about the durability of the U.S. lead was abruptly shaken last month by the Chinese company DeepSeek's release of its advanced artificial-intelligence model.

Adding to the risks is Beijing's ambition to absorb Taiwan, where 90% of the world's advanced computer chips are fabricated. China is gearing up for new rounds of economic warfare in other ways — for instance, denying American companies access to Chinese markets and banning the export to the U.S. of rare-earth materials, which are used in everything from semiconductors to batteries and weapons systems. To justify its ban, Beijing now deploys a term borrowed from the U.S., holding back rare-earth materials, it says, because of "dual use" — by the military as well as civilians.

Mr. Fishman acknowledges that, as countries seek to reduce their evident vulnerabilities, chokepoints can gradually lose their potency. Russia and China are trying to stick to rubles and yuan in their countries' burgeoning bilateral trade. A global embrace of cryptocurrencies could further reduce the primacy of the dollar.

With "Chokepoints," Mr. Fishman has provided a thorough analysis of what, for now, continues to be the power of that primacy. Curiously, however, the author does not address the many lessons of economic warfare learned during the Cold War, when the U.S. and its allies sought to control exports to the Soviet bloc. He also fails to define "neoliberal dogma," even though on occasion he joins the current fashion of deriding it — as if low deficits, low inflation, the avoidance of overregulation and the growth benefits of the global economy are all to be discounted. On Iran, he skims over the other powerful factor that brought the country to the negotiating table — the U.S. shale revolution. As the economic chokepoints were closing on Tehran, the physical valves were opening on the U.S. oil fields. Oil production surged, to the mullahs' disbelief, more than compensating for the Iranian oil that was being shut out of the market — making Iran's dire economic situation even worse.

"Chokepoints" appears at a moment when a new twist to economic warfare is at hand: Mr. Trump's penchant for tariffs, marking a reversion from the long trend toward trade liberalization that began with the revulsion against the trade wars of the 1930s. The second Trump administration is not hesitating to impose and brandish tariffs against both allies and adversaries, whether to offset trade deficits or to achieve other policy goals. At the same time, Moscow is already making clear in negotiations over its war against Ukraine that the removal of all sanctions is a primary objective.

Altogether, the current juncture makes "Chokepoints" a timely guide to the fragmenting of the global economy and the rising tensions that go with it. We are already in the age of economic warfare; the question is whether we can avoid an era of escalation.

  • Mr. Yergin, the vice chairman of S&P Global, is the author of "The Prize," "The Quest" and "The New Map."

data.title: NYMEX Overview: Gasoline, Oil Contract on Course for Week-to-Week Decline — OPIS
data.description:
Petroleum futures were trading sharply lower ahead of midday Friday and are on course to end the day with week-to-week declines as markets react to Trump administration efforts to boost global oil supply.

Crude oil and diesel contracts were off by about 2% as of 11 a.m. ET, while gasoline futures were about 1.8% lower.

ULSD contracts are still slightly above where they settled on Feb. 14, while crude and gasoline are both lower.

The declines follow reports that the White House is pressing Iraq to resume oil exports from the country's Kurdish region. The move is seen as an effort to offset supply losses from expected U.S. sanctions on Iran aimed at choking off oil exports from the country.

The NYMEX April West Texas Intermediate contract was down $1.55 to $70.93/bbl, leaving it 25cts below when it closed out last week. The May WTI contract was $1.53 lower at $70.76/bbl. The lightly traded April Brent contract was off by $1.49 to $74.99/bbl and the more-active May contract was $1.43 lower at $74.62/bbl.

Gasoline futures are more than 5cts below where they settled on Feb. 14, with the March RBOB contract down by 3.91cts to $2.0474/gal. The more-active April contract, which represents the more expensive summer-grade specification, was off by 3.83cts to $2.2834/gal.

The front-month ULSD contract remains about 0.25ct above last week's settlement even as it weakened by 5.36cts to $2.4498/gal. The more-active April contract was down 5.13cts to $2.3953/gal.

The possibility of increased exports from Iraq increases the uncertainty over global oil supply and demand.

These include whether OPEC and its allies will unwind voluntary production cuts later this year, whether a possible peace deal in Ukraine will increase Russian oil exports and the impact of Trump's plan to impose tariffs on goods from multiple U.S. trading partners.

While most of these factors have posed headwinds for prices, continued cold weather across much of the U.S. has helped to support ULSD prices even as oil and gasoline contracts weaken.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

  • Reporting by Steve Cronin, scronin@opisnet.com; Editing by Jeff Barber, jbarber@opisnet.com

data.title: UK Stocks Post Worst Week this Year
data.description:
The FTSE 100 ended almost flat on Friday after strong UK consumer spending data for January, but concerns about rising unemployment weighed on sentiment.

Consumer spending surged 1.7%, exceeding expectations, but business activity showed a slowdown, with companies cutting jobs.

The S&P Composite PMI for February slightly dipped to 50.5, signaling stagnation, and employment fell to its lowest since 2020.

Meanwhile, Natwest (3.8%) and Standard Chartered (3.4%) led gains, benefiting from strong earnings, while gold miners Fresnillo (-3.4%) and Endeavour (-3.2%) and oil companies like BP (-0.8%) and Shell (-1.5%) saw losses.

Traders are now pricing in limited rate cuts from the Bank of England amid weak economic growth and persistent inflation.

The index posted its worst weekly decline of the year, down nearly 1%.

data.title: FTSE 100 Closes Flat; StanChart Gains on FY24 Report
data.description:
British stocks closed the trading week little changed, with the FTSE 100 index down 0.03%, as the country's private sector saw another marginal growth in output thanks to services.

Flash data from S&P Global showed that the UK composite PMI fell to a two-month low of 50.5 in February, in line with market expectations, from 50.6 in January. The reading comes as service sector activity reached a two-month high, helping offset the 14-month low seen by the manufacturing sector.

"Early PMI survey data for February indicate that business activity remained largely stalled for a fourth successive month, with job losses mounting amid falling sales and rising costs," S&P Global Market Intelligence Chief Business Economist Chris Williamson said. "The lack of growth alongside rising price pressures points to a stagflationary environment which will present a growing dilemma for the Bank of England."

In other economic news, UK retail sales rose 1.7% month over month in January 2025, against the revised 0.6% fall in the previous month and the consensus estimate of 0.3% growth, data from the Office for National Statistics showed. The figure was mainly driven by food store sales, which saw the biggest increase since March 2020.

On the corporate front, Standard Chartered climbed 3.77% on Friday's close, the top gainer on the blue-chip index, after reporting a higher attributable profit and operating income for full-year 2024. The British bank will also launch a share buyback program worth $1.5 billion.

HSBC Holdings was up 0.68% as the British lender, Citigroup, Morgan Stanley and Royal Bank of Canada reached settlement deals with the UK Competition and Markets Authority over cases related to government bonds, or gilts. The companies agreed to pay 104.5 million pounds sterling in total fines by April 22. Deutsche Bank was exempt from fines after alerting the regulator to its participation.

"Following constructive engagement between the banks and the CMA, we are pleased that we have been able to settle these 5 cases involving the past sharing of competitively sensitive information about pricing," Juliette Enser, executive director for competition enforcement at the CMA, said. "The fines would have been substantially higher had the banks not already taken unusually extensive steps to make sure that this doesn't happen again."

data.title: UBS Downgrades TFI International to Neutral, Slashes Target to US$107, on LTL Improvement Doubts
data.description:
UBS has downgraded TFI International to Neutral, from Buy, and slashed its one year target to US$107, from US$163.

"We are downgrading TFI from Buy to Neutral because we no longer have visibility to a path of significant margin improvement in their U.S. LTL business." TFI's fourth-quarter U.S. LTL operating ratio of 97.3% was 630 bp worse y/y and shipments continue to fall -6%y/y, the brokerage notes.

"Considering the much worse than expected 4Q performance and TFI's commentary of continuing challenges in 1H25, we no longer have confidence in the core thesis of U.S. LTL margin improvement as a key lever for EPS growth."

UBS also noted that since TFI acquired UPS Freight in April 2021, its U.S. LTL shipment volume has fallen more than 30% and volumes continue to decline. "We believe managing the mix of shipments makes sense but we don't yet see clear evidence of stabilization in TFI's LTL shipments, let alone a meaningful move up in volume."

data.title: Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. PCE Data, German Elections in Focus
data.description:
Below are the most important global events likely to affect FX and bond markets in the week starting Feb. 24.

Focus for the week will center on U.S. PCE inflation data and any further news on President Trump's plans for trade tariffs.

In Europe, the results of German elections will be scrutinized, while in Asia, Japanese inflation data and a South Korean interest-rate decision are due.

U.S.

The Federal Reserve's preferred measure of inflation, the PCE price index for January, is due on Friday.

This comes after consumer price inflation for February was much higher than expected. Analysts say the components of this data--and the subsequent producer-price data--which feed into the core PCE measure were less strong. This suggests inflationary pressures might not be as concerning as the CPI data suggest.

Still, it is unlikely that this or any other U.S. data in the coming week will shake expectations that the Federal Reserve won't cut interest rates before fall at the earliest. U.S. money markets currently fully price a 25 basis-point rate cut for September.

"The bigger picture is that progress on lowering inflation has stalled in recent months, with the threat of various tariffs adding to concerns," Investec economist Philip Shaw said in a note.

Further data include the Conference Board's consumer confidence measure for February on Tuesday, followed by the second estimate of fourth-quarter gross domestic product, durable goods data for January and weekly jobless claims on Thursday.

Housing data include the Case-Shiller house price index on Tuesday and January new home sales on Wednesday.

In terms of politics, investors await further announcements from President Trump on his plans to implement hefty trade tariffs.

The Treasury will auction $69 billion 2-year notes on Monday, $70 billion in 5-year notes on Tuesday and $44 billion in 7-year notes on Wednesday.

CANADA

Canadian gross domestic product data for December and the fourth quarter are due Friday.

GDP should have been boosted by recent unexpectedly strong retail sales in December. However, analysts remain cautious about prospects for the economy going forward due to risks of the U.S. implementing hefty tariffs on Canadian imports.

EUROZONE

Markets will begin the week scrutinizing the results of Germany's elections on February 23.

Analysts say if major traditional parties fail to secure a two-thirds majority, this could lead to concerns about whether the new government will be able to agree on fiscal stimulus.

In terms of data, focus will center on the release of provisional inflation figures for February from Spain on Thursday, then France and Germany Friday.

"We expect [these data] to confirm a downward trend in inflation," analysts at Danske Bank said in a note.

Germany's Ifo business climate index on Monday could confirm the optimism in the latest ZEW sentiment index. The breakdown of fourth-quarter German GDP on Tuesday will provide insight into the strong and weak segments of the economy, while final French GDP is due on Friday.

The German GfK consumer sentiment index for February is due on Wednesday, alongside French consumer confidence.

Italy publishes business and consumer survey data on Thursday, followed by French consumer spending and German unemployment figures on Friday.

Germany will auction 1.5 billion euros in 2053-dated green Bunds on Tuesday and 2 billion euros in 2036- and 2038-dated Bunds on Wednesday. Other issuers include Belgium on Monday and Italy on Tuesday and Thursday.

HUNGARY

Hungary's central bank announces a decision on Tuesday and is expected to leave interest rates on hold at 6.50%.

SWITZERLAND

Swiss fourth-quarter gross domestic product data are due on Thursday.

U.K.

The coming week is a quiet one in the U.K., with just Nationwide house-price data for February due Friday.

The U.K. is due to auction 1.6 billion pounds in index-linked government bonds maturing in 2035 on Tuesday, then 3.25 billion pounds in government bonds maturing in 2040 on Thursday.

SCANDINAVIA

Sweden announces data on gross domestic product for the fourth quarter of 2024 on Friday.

Norway will hold a bond auction on Wednesday.

JAPAN

Consumer price data for Tokyo, scheduled to be released Friday, will likely show that inflation has stayed above the Bank of Japan's 2% target amid expectations for an additional interest-rate hike later this year.

The print is considered to be a leading indicator for nation-wide price trends. Core consumer prices excluding fresh food for the Tokyo metropolitan area are expected to have increased 2.3% in February from a year earlier, according to a poll of economists by data provider Quick. That compares with the 2.5% rise in January.

Figures for industrial production and retail sales for January are also due Friday. After the last batch of data for December showed a rebound in industrial output, the upcoming releases will be looked at for further evidence that Japan's economy is in good shape for another interest-rate hike.

The Ministry of Finance is scheduled to auction 2.6 trillion yen of two-year Japanese government bonds on Thursday. With the 2-year tenor highly sensitive to changes in expectations for the Bank of Japan's monetary-policy trajectory, investors may wait for lower price levels to buy the sovereign notes against the backdrop of rising bets for BOJ rate increases.

The Bank of Japan is scheduled to make outright purchases of JGBs on Wednesday, which could provide support for the domestic government bond market. The central bank plans to buy JGBs with tenors of more than 3 years and up to 5 years; more than 5 years and up to 10 years; and more than 10 years and up to 25 years. It also intends to buy inflation-indexed bonds.

Japanese markets are closed for a national holiday on Monday.

SOUTH KOREA

On Tuesday, the Bank of Korea is widely expected to resume its rate-cutting cycle to support fading economic growth.

All but one of the 27 economists in a Wall Street Journal survey expect the central bank to cut its base rate by 25 basis points to 2.75% at its policy meeting.

Growth remains weak, HSBC economist Jin Choi said, citing downside risks from rising U.S. tariffs. The volatile won-dollar trade last month let the BOK stand pat following back-to-back rate cuts in October and November. But the recent easing of forex volatility, combined with weakening growth and behaving inflation clear the way for the resumption of easing, Choi said.

The central bank is expected to meaningfully lower its 2025 growth forecast, he expects, with domestic demand likely delimited and tariffs casting uncertainty over exports.

Still, the BOK is likely to be extremely cautious in telegraphing more rate cuts, ING economist Min Joo Kang said. The central bank worries that cuts could speed up household debt and property prices, and is also keeping a close eye on how the widening gap between U.S. and Korea yields affects the won, she added.

AUSTRALIA

With the Reserve Bank of Australia telling markets that it is closely monitoring incoming data to determine if interest rates can fall further, attention will fix on the release of monthly inflation data for January on Wednesday.

Any rise in the inflation outlook might see markets push out bets on the next cut in interest rates to May, with the RBA remaining on hold at its next policy meeting in April.

The head of the RBA's economic analysis department, Michael Plumb, will give a speech on Thursday, but given all the central bank's communication in the last week, there is little scope for that to move markets.

The week will also see the release of data on construction work done and business investment in the fourth quarter, which will shape forecasts for gross domestic product growth in the quarter.

A modest recovery is expected in the economy over 2025.

CHINA

Official PMIs on Saturday, March 1, are the sole event on China's economic calendar.

After surprisingly weak prints in January, observers will look for signs of improvement in the February gauges of manufacturing, services and construction activity. Focus will be on a pickup in construction amid concerns about sluggish house building, as well as on export orders and production activity amid worries about trade tariffs.

Analysts at Citi Research expect the February manufacturing PMI to return to an expansionary 50.2, in part due to post-Lunar New Year seasonality.

Attention remains on U.S. tariff announcements and Beijing's responses, especially as expectations build ahead of the key Two Sessions meetings--the annual plenary sessions of two major political bodies--in March where China will likely announce more stimulus.

Without additional fiscal and monetary support, it will be hard for growth in consumption to offset the dual pressures of slowing investment and exports, said Alicia Garcia Herrero, Asia-Pacific chief economist at Natixis CIB.

"Fiscal announcements during the Two Sessions and the stance of the PBOC regarding potential interest rate cuts will be critical factors to monitor," she said.

Any moves by the People's Bank of China regarding the yuan will also be closely watched.

The central bank seems to be holding on to the currency tightly for now, wary of giving investors reason to think it might soften, said Thomas Mathews, head of APAC markets at Capital Economics. Weakening the yuan too obviously could stoke the ire of trading partners--particularly the U.S.--but Mathews thinks the PBOC will eventually allow depreciation to help offset the effect of tariffs and free up space for further monetary easing to support the economy.

HONG KONG

Hong Kong will deliver its budget on Wednesday, alongside revised economic growth figures for the fourth quarter of 2024.

Markets will be watching to see if officials deliver more measures to revive sluggish consumer demand and lift lackluster economic growth. The prior budget featured measures like an easing of property-market restrictions to breathe life back into the moribund sector, as well as a scaling back of stimulus spending to focus more on fiscal consolidation.

PwC expects Hong Kong to record a HK$94.8 billion consolidated budget deficit for the fiscal year, and looks for the budget to offer taxpayers support as living costs rise.

Any mention of measures to shore up the financial hub in the face of rising external headwinds--headlined by the slowdown of the closely linked Chinese economy and U.S. tariffs--will be in focus.

A government spokesman said in early February that the economy is expected to grow in 2025 despite heightened external uncertainties and the potential tariff hit to Hong Kong's exports. The government introduced several measures in October, including looser mortgage restrictions to improve the housing market, and liquor-tax cuts to aid tourism and related industries.

INDIA

India reports growth figures on Friday, Feb. 28, which will likely show a modest sequential pickup in activity but remain below its full economic potential, making the case for continued policy support, the ANZ Research team said.

A faster growth in construction likely boosted investment growth, but consumer spending likely remained weak, ANZ said. ANZ pegs fiscal third-quarter GDP growth at 6.3% on the year, up from 5.4% in the previous quarter.

Rahul Bajoria of BofAS India also thinks that the October-December print will show a gradual recovery in economic growth, led by supply-side and industrial indicators amid soft consumption.

The biggest drag on consumption appears to be low wage growth, a hurdle to any pickup in spending, the economist said in a note. More fiscal spending and policy easing by the Reserve Bank of India will take some time to revive growth later in 2025, he added.

However, in recent days, "we sense that confidence around growth revival is growing," Bajoria and FX strategist Claudio Piron said. Even though global factors remain uncertain, the biggest turning point for sentiment appears to be domestic fiscal and monetary policy, with a gradual, consistent recovery expected thanks to greater policy coordination, and more stability, they said.

THAILAND

The Bank of Thailand will make its interest-rate decision Wednesday, where it could hold policy settings steady.

The odds are tilted in favor of a dovish pause, said Sanjay Mathur, chief economist for Southeast Asia and India at ANZ. A lackluster recovery and benign inflation keeps the door open for a rate cut, but the BOT may prefer to preserve its policy buffer and await more clarity on U.S. trade policy, ANZ added.

The Thai central bank could also consider a different kind of easing, HSBC Global Research economist Aris Dacanay said. The BOT, in cooperation with fiscal authorities, is set to implement a debt-relief program for households and small- and medium-sized enterprises, Dacanay said.

Domestic banks will partially absorb the cost of the program through net interest margins, he added. "A cut in the policy rate right when the program commences may be a blow to the NIM of banks, enough to make the BOT cautious in loosening the monetary reins," Dacanay said.

SINGAPORE

On Monday, Singapore will release its consumer inflation data for January. Market participants will watch to see whether core inflation slowed further last month, after December's 1.8% expansion.

A continuous slowdown in Singapore's core inflation could back the case for the Monetary Authority of Singapore to further loosen its monetary policy stance, which is centered on the city-state's exchange rate. Late last month, the central bank eased policy by slightly reducing the slope of the Singapore dollar nominal effective exchange rate policy band while keeping the width and midpoint of the band unchanged.

Core inflation is likely to have eased due to base effects from last year's goods and services tax hike, Barclays analysts said in a note.

-Any references to days are in local times.

  • Additional reporting by Megumi Fujikawa, Kwanwoo Jun, Amanda Lee, Ronnie Harui, Kimberley Kao, James Glynn, Emese Bartha and Renae Dyer

Write to Jessica Fleetham at jessica.fleetham@wsj.com and Fabiana Negrin Ochoa at fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com

data.title: Update: Lundin Gold Up 4% as Q4 Adjusted Earnings Surge, Boosts Dividend
data.description:
(Updates shares.)

Lundin Gold late on Thursday reported a jump in its fourth-quarter adjusted earnings on higher revenue and production.

The company's adjusted earnings, excluding most one-time items, rose to US$129.1 million, or US$0.54 per share, up from US$33.2 million, or US$0.14, in the year-prior quarter.

Revenue rose to US$341.8 million from US$190.7 million. Adjusted EBITDA was US$232.2 million, jumping from US$95.9 million.

The company produced 135,241 gold ounces, up from 99,310 gold ounces, in the fourth quarter of 2023.

Lundin Gold maintained its 2025 guidance for gold production of between 475,000 and 525,000 ounces.

Separately, Lundin Gold boosted its quarterly cash dividend to US$0.30 per share from US$0.20 per common share.

The dividend will be paid on March 26 to shareholders of record on March 11.

The board has also approved the company's notice of intention to launch a normal course issuer bid, which has been accepted by the TSX.

Under the NCIB, Lundin may buyback and cancel up to 12-million shares, 5% of its issued and outstanding common shares as of Feb. 11.

Purchases can be made during over 12 months starting Feb. 25, and ending Feb. 24, 2026, or when it reaches the maximum amount permitted under the NCIB.

Lundin Gold shares were last seen up $1.69 to $40.06 on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

data.title: 'Two Sisters' Review: Help On the Run
data.description:
By Diane Cole

Why did a stranger "stand up to evil" to save her? The Holocaust survivor Huguette Müller began pondering that question in the winter of 1943 as a 15-year-old German-Jewish refugee in Nazi-occupied France. While she and her sister, Marion, were attempting to flee, Huguette slipped on the ice and injured her leg so severely that the girls were forced to stay in the small Alpine village of Val d'Isère. Because the Gestapo monitored hospitals for Jews and Resistance members, the sisters turned to the town physician, Frédéric Pétri, for help. Correctly sensing from the sisters' fear that they were Jews, he offered to care for Huguette in secret in his home for the six months it would take her to heal and walk again.

In 2019, Huguette began to disclose the details of her survival in Nazi France to her late sister's daughter-in-law, the journalist Rosie Whitehouse, who soon made it her mission to uncover the secrets about the sisters' precarious existence on the run from the Gestapo. The result is Ms. Whitehouse's evocative and wrenching family history, "Two Sisters: Betrayal, Love and Resistance in Wartime France."

The author's detective work reveals that, unbeknownst to Huguette or to other family members, Marion had been an active member of the Resistance. So, too, had Pétri. Tracking down Pétri's daughter, Ms. Whitehouse further discovered that the doctor had grown up in a French family in Alsace-Lorraine before World War I, while the region was under German rule. Until France regained control of the area in 1919, the French language had been banned by Germany, creating lasting resentment among loyal French speakers, like Pétri's family. Growing up this way, Ms. Whitehouse suggests, Pétri "had learned how it felt when there was no acceptance that people are different."

Ms. Whitehouse also learned of Pétri's rescue in 1944 of a British soldier who had nearly frozen to death in a snowdrift. With abundant evidence of Pétri's selflessness in hand, Ms. Whitehouse spearheaded a successful campaign to have Israel's Holocaust memorial Yad Vashem name him Righteous Among Nations, a title given to non-Jews who risk their lives to save Jews. The recognition is uplifting, but for Ms. Whitehouse and her readers, the shadows of France's Nazi occupation and the Holocaust remain.

  • Ms. Cole is the author of the memoir "After Great Pain: A New Life Emerges."

data.title: Gold Set for Eighth Consecutive Week of Gains on Strong Safe-Haven Demand
data.description:
By Joe Hoppe

Gold futures are set to end their eighth consecutive week higher on persistent economic uncertainty and safe-haven demand, after setting a fresh record.

Continuous gold futures on the New York Mercantile Exchange set a fresh record of $2,973.40 a troy ounce on Thursday. As of Friday European evening trading, futures are up 1.45% on week, and have risen more than 11% in the year-to-date.

Several factors have driven up the price of the yellow metal in recent weeks.

High levels of safe-haven demand driven by President Trump's tariff strategy and recent developments in Ukraine have underpinned much of the recent price gains, said Philip Newman, managing director of precious metals consultancy Metals Focus.

Trump plans a 25% levy on all steel and aluminum imports, has floated tariffs on automobiles, semiconductors and pharmaceutical products, and threatened reciprocal levies on any country imposing its own tariffs on U.S. products.

U.S. consumer confidence slumped in February, according to the University of Michigan's index of consumer sentiment, reflecting expectations that the tariffs will lead to rising prices. A 25% tariff on Canada and Mexico was delayed by one month after reaching agreements with the U.S. in early February.

Tensions are also escalating between Europe and the U.S. after Trump called Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky a dictator and said Russia's invasion of the country was Ukraine's fault, further increasing safe-haven demand for bullion.

The strong underlying momentum in gold's rally and the "fear of missing out" on gold's climb are being augmented by persistent end-user demand from central banks and individual investors seeking protection in an increasingly unstable-looking world, Saxo Bank's Ole Hansen said in a note.

The lack of investor selling back is a key factor in gold's climb, with institutional investors reluctant to liquidate their holdings in the current environment, Newman said.

The past week saw demand for bullion-backed exchange-traded funds on-track for the biggest weekly rise in holdings since 2023. In the past three trading days, the world's largest gold ETF has seen inflows of more than 20 metric tons, Commerzbank analysts said in a note.

"This is a sign that investors are increasingly prepared to pay up in order to get exposure to a rally that has set its sails towards $3,000, and most likely beyond," Hansen said.

That said, before the $3,000 an ounce mark can be breached, the market will likely focus on incoming U.S. economic data for further clues on the pathway of the U.S. dollar and monetary policy easing, Hansen added.

Next Wednesday will see the release of U.S. Initial Jobless Claims data, followed by the Personal Consumption Expenditure index on Friday.

Write to Joe Hoppe at joseph.hoppe@wsj.com

data.title: CK Infrastructure Makes Preliminary 7 Billion Pound Bid for Majority of Thames Water, the FT Says, Citing Sources
data.description:
• Hong Kong's CK Infrastructure submitted a preliminary offer for a majority stake in debt-laden utility Thames Water earlier this month, The Financial Times reports, citing two people familiar with the matter.
  • CK Infrastructure, part of CK Hutchison group, said it would be willing to inject 7 billion pounds ($8.87 billion) of equity to mitigate Thames Water's debt burden, but would require bondholders of the company's debt to accept significant writedowns, the individuals added, according to The Financial Times.
  • CK Infrastructure's 75% stake in another regional water monopoly means a potential deal with Thames Water may cause issues with competition regulators, The Financial Times reports.
  • Thames Water declined the FT's request for comment. A representative for CKI did not immediately respond to a FT request for comment.

Full story: https://bit.ly/3EPP9FZ

Write to Andrea Figueras at andrea.figueras@wsj.com

data.title: Akamai Worries Investors as TikTok Exposure Set to Diminish Revenue
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Akamai Technologies revealed to investors that TikTok is a much larger customer than many expected, fueling pessimism about 2025 earnings as the social media platform plans to transition operations internally and faces a ban in the U.S.

Investors dropped the stock, which was the worst performer on the S&P 500 Friday morning, after Akamai said exposure to TikTok and plans to exit some smaller businesses are expected to dampen revenue growth over the next couple years.

The Cambridge, Mass., cloud computing company said Thursday that TikTok is its largest customer, making up about 6% of total revenue. That news was "the surprise of the night" for investors, Bank of America analysts said in a note, and worried investors since the social media app is transitioning away from its partnership with Akamai.

Akamai has provided a content delivery network to TikTok, but the short-video platform is starting to source that service internally, said D.A. Davidson analyst Rudy Kessinger. That is likely to drag down revenue growth by 1 percentage point both this year and next, and could drag it down by 2 points if the site is banned, Kessinger said.

Revenue for the year is expected to expand around 2.7%, missing Wall Street's estimates of a 6.8% increase, according to FactSet.

Akamai did say Thursday it closed a deal to offer computing services to TikTok, which is expected to bring in $100 million over a multiyear period. In 2025, it is expected to earn $5 million to $10 million, but analysts are concerned that Akamai may have given TikTok concessions on its delivery services to secure the deal, potentially offsetting some of that revenue, Kessinger said.

Bank of America analysts said Akamai has opportunities for long-term growth, but they don't expect it to return to double-digit growth until 2029 at the earliest.

Overall, Akamai is transitioning its focus to cloud infrastructure services and cybersecurity. Security and compute revenue grew 15% in the fourth quarter, but growth this year is expected to be masked by the company's plan to exit some of its more niche businesses such as video manager, image and next storage. That exit is likely to hit revenue growth by 10%, Oppenheimer analysts said.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: CAC 40 Rises as Investors Weigh PMI Data and Earnings
data.description:
The CAC 40 rose 0.4% to close at 8,155 on Friday, marking a second consecutive session of gains as investors assessed flash PMI data from Europe alongside corporate earnings.

Eurozone private sector activity steadied, with France weighing on growth while Germany showed improvement.

France’s composite PMI for February signaled the sharpest contraction since September 2023, as services slumped to a 17-month low and manufacturing remained in negative territory.

On the corporate front, Air Liquide led gains, rising 3.3% on strong annual results and an upward guidance revision, while STMicroelectronics climbed 1.4% after unveiling a new photonics chip for AI data centers in collaboration with Amazon Web Services.

Conversely, Airbus sank 3.1% following weaker-than-expected full-year guidance, fresh charges in its Space and defense divisions, and persistent supply chain issues.

Despite Friday’s gains, the CAC 40 ended the week down 0.2%, snapping a six-week winning streak.

data.title: KeyBanc: Nvidia Expected to Beat Earnings Estimates as Demand Offsets Supply Constraints
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA, Financials) is expected to exceed fiscal fourth-quarter earnings estimates, according to KeyBanc, with analysts anticipating strong performance despite ongoing manufacturing challenges.

The company forecasts a modest rise in fiscal first-quarter guidance, somewhat above average estimates.

Production problems with Nvidia's GB200 NVL servers still cause supply restrictions as lower initial yields limit shipments. The corporation is reducing the effect, however, by satisfying demand with HGX-based B200 servers depending on x86 head nodes.

DeepSeek's growth and a scarcity of Huawei's Ascend AI processors have prompted demand for Nvidia's H20 GPUs to rise in China. Furthermore, clients of Nvidia's cloud service providers are funding inventories at electronic manufacturing companies, therefore enabling the corporation to record sell-in shipments as income.

In premarket trade, Nvidia shares climbed 1% on the news. As of 11:27 a.m. GMT-5 on Friday, Nvidia shares were trading at $139.10, down 0.7% for the day. Reiterating his Overweight recommendation on the company, KeyBanc analyst John Vinh increased the price objective to $190 from $180 ahead of the next results release.

Following the market closure, Nvidia is scheduled to unveil its quarterly earnings results on Feb. 26.

data.title: Vicor Shares Climb Following 4Q Earnings, Revenue Beat
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Shares of Vicor hit a 52-week high after the company posted fourth-quarter results that surpassed analyst expectations.

The stock was recently up 23% at $64, having retreated slightly from its earlier high of $64.50. Shares have climbed 79% in the past year.

The Andover, Mass., company, which develops and manufactures modular power components and complete power systems, after the bell Thursday reported net income of $10.2 million, up from $8.7 million a year earlier.

Quarterly earnings came in at 23 cents a share, topping the 19 cents that analysts surveyed by FactSet were expecting.

Revenue increased to $96.2 million from $92.7 million. Analysts had forecast sales of $91 million.

Chief Executive Patrizio Vinciarelli said licensing has gained traction, driven by more companies adopting Vicor's patented high-density power-system solutions.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: Rivian's Nightmare Recall: 17,260 EVs at Risk--Investors Are Hitting the Brakes
data.description:
Rivian just hit another bump in the roadthis time, it's a recall of 17,260 vehicles over a headlight malfunction that could make night driving a gamble in cold weather. The issue? The low beams on certain 2025 R1S SUVs and R1T pickups might not turn on when starting the vehicle, increasing the risk of accidents. Rivian is swapping out the faulty headlight control modules for free, blaming the problem on incorrectly configured supplier parts. While the company says there have been no reported accidents or injuries, this recall adds to the growing list of challenges Rivian is navigating as it works toward profitability.

This comes at a rough moment. Rivian recently warned of a surprise drop in 2025 deliveries, even as it aggressively cuts costs to reach profitability. The company is betting big on slashing raw material and supply chain expenses to stay in the game, but recalls like this put pressure on its quality control. Scaling an EV business isn't just about efficiencyit's about keeping customers confident in the product. And with competition in the electric truck and SUV space heating up, Rivian can't afford too many missteps.

Investors aren't thrilled. Rivian's stock dropped 4% at 11.18am today. While the financial hit from the recall itself may be minimal, it feeds into a larger narrative: Rivian is still proving it can balance growth, cost-cutting, and product reliability all at once. The big question now? Whether the company's drive for efficiency keeps it on trackor sends it skidding off course.

data.title: Kellanova Keeps Dividend at $0.57 Per Share, Payable March 14 to Shareholders of Record on March 3
